## Cases mentioned in passing

### Hydraulic cylinder failure at 7,500 PSI
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Hydraulic cylinder failure at 7,500 PSI"
- **Anchor:** `§1.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "State what the problem is. That's one overhead. The other nine are basically what's the solution. Things students have done: pole-vaulting poles — one year I had two students do pole-vaulting poles, and it's an interesting composite material, has to be very flexible, has to be very strong, has to be very light. Tennis racquets. Gothic arches like the flying buttresses on cathedrals from 800 years ago in Europe. Sailing hardware. I don't want how to build an America's Cup yacht — that's too complex. But you could have, like one of my lectures, the failure of a 7,500 psi hydraulic cylinder that..."

### US Navy Ship Design and Japanese Steel Technology
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "US Navy Ship Design and Japanese Steel Technology"
- **Anchor:** `§2.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "We're in an engineering class. There's nothing wrong with using someone else's good idea — you can present it as long as you attribute it to the person you got it from. I spent a year in Yokohama, my first sabbatical in Japan, and Japanese steel technology is used all over the world, because you'd be stupid not to use the best technology. Some people consider it stealing. It depends. It is stealing if it's patented. But the Japanese aren't necessarily stealing patented technology. They would say they're using the best technology and you're foolish not to use it. On the other hand, the..."

### Medical laser patent consulting case
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Medical laser patent consulting case"
- **Anchor:** `§3.p4`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "I mentioned the academics in the arts and sciences often look down on engineering. I gave you the quote from Francis Amasa Walker, the president of MIT in 1911, where he says engineers have too long been thought of by other academics in the arts and sciences as having attained their accomplishments by some lower intellectual merit. I gave you the von Kármán quote: a scientist explains that which exists, and an engineer creates that which never was. There was also a quote that scientists like to create new knowledge, things we didn't understand before. An engineer actually likes to put things..."

### Classmate's career transition from Goldman Sachs to preventive medicine
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Classmate's career transition from Goldman Sachs to preventive medicine"
- **Anchor:** `§4.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "Which brings me to another story. I always have stories. One of my classmates as an undergraduate went off to Stanford Business School, got hired by Goldman Sachs, went off to Southern California, became a broker for Goldman Sachs, became a partner. He was making nearly a million bucks a year. This was in the early 1980s. He finally decided when he was about thirty or thirty-two he couldn't continue. He went to his billion-dollar clients, had lunch with them to explain he was going to move back to Boston, go to Harvard School of Public Health, get a doctorate in preventive medicine. They..."

### D-Day artificial harbors
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "D-Day artificial harbors"
- **Anchor:** `§8.p4`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "Brigadier General S.L.A. Marshall — this is not George Marshall of the Marshall Plan — in his eloquent address at the giving of the Hoover Medal, said: to dig the Panama Canal and free it of rock slides over many years required the movement of seventy-two million tons of earth. Not pounds — seventy-two million tons. That's a lot of rock. The Suez Canal, like that. The United Kingdom moved even more, transported across oceans in the face of enemy peril, to artificial harbors — this is for D-Day. Omaha Beach. They built a whole docking system and moved it across the English Channel for D-Day...."
